articles.title: ‘It’s expensive’: Syria’s electricity has improved, but challenges remain
articles.summary: Damascus residents say state-supplied power has noticeably improved recently but complain about the cost of energy.
articles.titleZh: “价格昂贵”：叙利亚电力有所改善，但挑战依然存在
articles.summaryZh: 大马士革居民表示，国家供电最近明显改善，但对能源成本有所抱怨。

contentParagraphs.en: Damascus, Syria – Nasri Tadros sweeps dust away from the electric scooters parked outside his small shop in central Damascus.“I have three generators for my shop, and this runs on batteries,” he said, pointing to a battery-operated lighter he uses to fire up charcoal. He stepped into the narrow shop that he rents, where he has worked for the past two years, selling and delivering products for hookahs.Around Damascus, solar panels and diesel generators are a common sight. During the harshest years of the war, they supplemented the sparse state-provided electricity, which only came a few hours a day between long cuts.As Damascus enters the summer months, there is concern about the dry heat, which reaches the high 30s in Celsius (up to almost 100F). However, locals say that while cuts are still common, electricity has recently improved to five or six straight hours of supply before a cut.“Nothing can work without electricity,” Tadros said, the air conditioning blasting away inside his modest store.ProgressBy the end of the dynastic rule of the Assad regime – father Hafez (1970-2000) and son Bashar (2000-24) – Syria was left in ruins. A popular 2011 uprising was brutally repressed by Bashar and the country slowly crumbled until a rebel operation forced the longtime ruling family into exile.Bashar al-Assad’s departure in late 2024 was met with great joy by many Syrians, but he left behind a challenge for Syria’s liberators. The country’s economy is in dire straits, and state infrastructure, including the electricity sector, requires serious rebuilding.As of 2023, before the fall of the Assad regime, Syria received 52 percent of its electricity from natural gas and close to 45 percent from oil, according to the International Energy Agency (IEA). Only 3 percent came from hydropower and even less from solar.In June 2025, the World Bank approved a $146m grant to improve Syria’s electricity supply and to develop the sector. When the government of President Ahmed al-Sharaa moved into northeast Syria earlier this year, it took over the country’s largest oil-producing region.During the war, the area, which was controlled by the Kurdish-led Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF), produced about 10,000 barrels of oil per day. Now, it is about 100,000bpd and could reach up to 200,000bpd by the end of the year. After the Syrian army’s offensive, the government and the SDF reached a ceasefire.The Syrian government has also secured gas import deals with countries like Azerbaijan, Jordan and Egypt, and in May 2025 signed a $7bn energy deal with Qatari, Turkish and US firms. Much of this progress has come about thanks to Western sanctions relief, which has been a priority of the new government.“Consumer tariffs have been restructured, and solar power is helping, albeit modestly, to fill the gap,” John Calabrese, a nonresident senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, told Al Jazeera.Remaining challengesHowever, the gap remains. Almost everyone interviewed about Syria’s electricity situation turned the conversation towards affordability and the high cost of alternative energy.“I would be lying if I said electricity was not improving,” a shop owner, who asked his name be withheld because he was critical of the new government, told Al Jazeera. “I have solar energy,” he added. “But it’s expensive.”While solar panels have become a more regular sight in Damascus, solar energy has a high initial investment cost, which means many low-income families, with almost 90 percent of the country below the poverty line, cannot afford it.“Fuel supplies remain the core bottleneck,” Calabrese said. Syria has been suffering from fuel shortages of late, causing long queues at petrol stations. The energy minister announced on Saturday that supplies of diesel and petrol were being increased, and distribution accelerated.Other issues also remain. Some reports say the Syrian Petroleum Company is facing internal tensions between the CEO and the board.“Bureaucratic and governance friction is an obstacle to further progress,” Calabrese said.Back at the corner store, the shop owner who requested anonymity animatedly waved his arms in the air. “In the first world, electricity is a right,” he said in thick Damascene-accented Arabic. “Here, it is a dream.”
contentParagraphs.zh: 叙利亚大马士革 — 纳斯里·塔德罗斯 (Nasri Tadros) 正在清扫停在大马士革市中心小店外的电动滑板车上的灰尘。“我的店里有三台发电机，都是用电池供电的，”他指着一个用来点燃木炭的电池驱动打火机说道。他走进自己租用的狭窄商店，过去两年他一直在那里工作，销售和运送水烟产品。在大马士革周围，太阳能电池板和柴油发电机随处可见。在战争最艰难的几年里，他们补充了国家提供的稀缺电力，这些电力在长时间停电之间每天只供应几个小时。随着大马士革进入夏季，人们对高达 30 摄氏度（高达近 100 华氏度）的干热天气感到担忧。然而，当地人表示，虽然停电仍然很常见，但电力最近已改善到停电前连续供应五到六个小时。“没有电，一切都无法运转。”塔德罗斯说，他那间简陋的商店里的空调正在猛烈地运转。进展阿萨德政权——父亲哈菲兹（1970-2000年）和儿子巴沙尔（2000-24年）的王朝统治结束时，叙利亚已成为一片废墟。 2011 年的一场民众起义遭到巴沙尔残酷镇压，国家慢慢崩溃，直到一场叛乱行动迫使长期统治家族流亡。巴沙尔·阿萨德于 2024 年底下台，许多叙利亚人欣喜若狂，但他给叙利亚解放者留下了挑战。该国经济陷入困境，包括电力部门在内的国家基础设施需要认真重建。 根据国际能源署 (IEA) 的数据，截至 2023 年，阿萨德政权垮台之前，叙利亚 52% 的电力来自天然气，近 45% 来自石油。只有 3% 来自水力发电，来自太阳能的更少。 2025 年 6 月，世界银行批准了一笔 1.46 亿美元的赠款，用于改善叙利亚的电力供应并发展该行业。今年早些时候，艾哈迈德·萨拉总统的政府进入叙利亚东北部，接管了该国最大的石油产区。战争期间，该地区由库尔德人领导的叙利亚民主力量(SDF)控制，每天生产约1万桶石油。目前产量约为 10 万桶/日，到年底可能达到 20 万桶/日。叙利亚军队进攻后，政府与自卫队达成停火。叙利亚政府还与阿塞拜疆、约旦和埃及等国达成天然气进口协议，并于2025年5月与卡塔尔、土耳其和美国公司签署了价值70亿美元的能源协议。这些进展在很大程度上要归功于西方制裁的放松，这一直是新政府的首要任务。中东研究所非常驻高级研究员约翰·卡拉布雷斯告诉半岛电视台：“消费者关税已经重组，太阳能正在帮助填补这一缺口，尽管幅度不大。”几乎所有接受有关叙利亚电力状况采访的人都将话题转向可负担性和替代能源的高成本。“如果我说电力没有改善，那我就是在撒谎，”一位商店老板告诉半岛电视台。由于批评新政府，他要求隐去姓名。 “我有太阳能，”他补充道。 “但价格昂贵。”虽然太阳能电池板在大马士革已成为常见现象，但太阳能的初始投资成本很高，这意味着该国近 90% 的地区处于贫困线以下的许多低收入家庭无力承担。“燃料供应仍然是核心瓶颈，”卡拉布雷斯说。叙利亚近来一直遭受燃料短缺的困扰，导致加油站排起长队。能源部长周六宣布，正在增加柴油和汽油的供应，并加速分配。其他问题也仍然存在。一些报道称，叙利亚石油公司正面临首席执行官和董事会之间的内部紧张关系。卡拉布雷斯说：“官僚和治理摩擦是进一步发展的障碍。”回到街角商店，要求匿名的店主在空中热情地挥舞着双臂。 “在第一个世界，电力是一种权利，”他用浓重的大马士革口音的阿拉伯语说道。 “在这里，这是一个梦。”

articles.summary:

Every weekday, Matt, a software engineer, looks forward to his four-hour train commute to Pawling, New York. It’s t
articles.titleZh: 追求新技能，回归基础，推动集体行动：软件工程师如何适应人工智能
articles.summaryZh:
软件工程是 2022 年美国薪酬最高的职业之一，但人工智能的出现扰乱了它，导致多人裁员和就业不足
每个工作日，软件工程师马特（Matt）都期待着乘坐四个小时的火车前往纽约州波林（Pawling）。这是

contentParagraphs.en: Software engineering was one of the best-paying professions in the US in 2022, but the advent of AI has disrupted it, leading to several layoffs and underemploymentEvery weekday, Matt, a software engineer, looks forward to his four-hour train commute to Pawling, New York. It’s time he uses to work on his own project: a browser-based video game for which he writes every line of code himself.“I am actively trying to keep my axe sharp,” said Matt, who did not want to use his actual name, to protect his employment. In the last six months, Matt’s job has increasingly shifted away from coding, problem solving and software architecture towards reviewing code generated by artificial intelligence. Convinced that the shift will weaken his skills, he’s doing what he can to keep them intact. “I am trying not to leverage AI where I can.”His career as a software engineer, which typically has paid more than $200,000 annually, used to feel like a sure thing. But after a layoff last summer and a warning from his current boss to use AI more, he said his future feels dark.For a generation of workers like Matt, software engineering promised stability, security and upward mobility. But as AI changes how software is developed – Google says 75% of its code is now written by AI – it’s altering the profession much faster than the rank-and-file anticipated. Software engineers are frustrated, anxious and trying to adapt to a startling new reality in which the value of their skills is unclear. As a result, they’re doubling down on fundamentals, chasing new skills to stay relevant, seeking collective action to push for better protections or even contemplating exiting the industry entirely, according to more than a dozen software engineers who spoke to the Guardian.Software engineering was one of the biggest and best-paying professions in the US in 2022, with 1.5 million practitioners earning twice the national median salary, according to the US Bureau of Labor Statistics. Pay had risen amid escalating talent wars, during which companies offered bonuses of up to hundreds of thousands of dollars to snag and retain top programmers. Last year, nearly 50 million people worldwide worked as developers, according to market research firm SlashData.‘I’m trying to code it by hand,’ said Matt, who is not only coding his first game ‘from scratch’, but also designing his own 3D characters. Photograph: Oliver Farshi/The GuardianBut since the release of OpenAI’s ChatGPT in 2022, more than 600,000 US tech workers have lost their jobs, according to tech layoff tracker Layoff.fyi. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate for computer science graduates rose to 7% in 2024, up from 6.1% the previous year, and their underemployment rate was more than 19%, data from the New York Fed shows. US tech job postings on Indeed also dropped 36% from 2020 to 2025.Experts are unsure what’s next for software engineers, but they agree on one thing: coding skills may be losing value, but the ability to evaluate AI-written code is becoming more important.“It’s hard to say what exactly the profession will look like in two years, but it’s clear that the skill of writing code is over,” Bouke Klein Teeselink, assistant professor of economics at King’s College London, told the Guardian. “AI is hugely augmenting what it means to be a software engineer, and the reasonable yardstick” for success depends on how well engineers use the tech.Software engineers still have a job to do, AI has just changed it, said Ethan Mollick, an associate professor of management at the University of Pennsylvania’s Wharton School and author of the upcoming book Co-Existence.“Now it’s not about who can write the most code,” he said. Instead, focus is on defining problems, designing systems and directing AI tools effectively, he said. “It shifts the skills around, so suddenly that’s where the value is.”Before AI, Matt said he was a “leading voice” in a solution’s execution, but now “the line between what I am deciding and what AI provides has certainly blurred.”Adapting to the AI-eraSoftware engineers are grappling with a daunting choice: soldier on in an increasingly unpredictable industry or seek an alternative path.George Dover, a software engineer of six years in Portland, Oregon, became a substitute kindergarten teacher while he searched for new roles after a layoff at Inuit Mailchimp in late 2024.“It’s very difficult to let go of something that was a large part of your personality for a good number of years,” he said. “What else is there for me?”But Dover didn’t give up. Realizing the need to understand AI, he used AI to generate code to build websites and then evaluated it to learn its strengths and limitations. He checked the code for errors, redundancies, unusual AI decisions, bugs and visual glitches.“The quality has to be rigorously tested,” he said. “Sometimes that tradeoff is a good one, other times it leads you down rabbit holes that take longer than coding it yourself.”George Dover, a software engineer of six years, was laid off in 2024 and spent the time diving into AI. 400 applications and several interviews later, he recently landed a software engineering job oriented toward AI. Photograph: Leah Nash/The GuardianIt paid off. Almost two years after being laid off, 400 applications and several interviews later, Dover landed a software engineering job – one oriented towards AI.Dover isn’t the only one experimenting. More non-technical workers are writing code, expanding overall output, which could drive demand for software engineers, said Teeselink. Validating AI-written code requires someone to “find vulnerabilities, understand errors, check security, etc., which non-coders will not be able to do”, he said.But it’s too early to make any definitive declarations about the profession, especially since AI only started generating good quality code last year, said Shriram Krishnamurthi, professor of computer science at Brown University. Still, the growing need for code reviews will

articles.title: Campaign text messages could soon get more effective — and annoying
articles.summary: Taught to sound like a candidate, bots are engaging voters with personalized text messages, making AI-generated texting conversations the latest tool political campaigns are using to connect.
articles.titleZh: 竞选短信很快就会变得更加有效——但也很烦人
articles.summaryZh: 机器人被训练得像候选人一样，通过个性化短信与选民互动，使人工智能生成的短信对话成为政治竞选活动用来联系的最新工具。

contentParagraphs.en: In this file photo, a man using a smartphone passes through the arched windows at Grand Central Terminal in New York City on March 24, 2025.
contentParagraphs.zh: 在这张照片中，一名男子使用智能手机于2025年3月24日穿过纽约市中央车站的拱形窗户。

contentParagraphs.en: Stay up to date with our Politics newsletter, sent weekly.
contentParagraphs.zh: 及时了解我们每周发送的政治新闻通讯。

contentParagraphs.en: AI-powered platforms are training bots to sound like political candidates in text messages, holding personalized conversations with thousands of potential voters simultaneously. The bots are also gathering data, learning what each voter wants from their representatives and using that information to shape future campaign messaging.
contentParagraphs.zh: 人工智能平台正在训练机器人，使其在短信中听起来像政治候选人，同时与数千名潜在选民进行个性化对话。这些机器人也在收集数据，了解每个选民想要从他们的代表那里得到什么，并利用这些信息来塑造未来的竞选信息。

contentParagraphs.en: Aaron Sheeks, the CEO of Akillion, an AI platform that lets people run their own Large Language Models or bots, said many of his current clients are running for political office.
contentParagraphs.zh: 让人们运行自己的大型语言模型或机器人的人工智能平台Akillion的首席执行官Aaron Sheeks表示，他目前的许多客户都在竞选政治职位。

contentParagraphs.en: "Our goal is to put the microphone back in the hand of the voter," said Sheeks. "We're giving agencies and political campaigns the ability to have a trained AI employee that can go back and forth and answer questions on police reform or education or tax changes."
contentParagraphs.zh: Sheeks说： “我们的目标是将麦克风放回选民手中。” “我们让各机构和政治运动有能力拥有受过训练的人工智能员工，他们可以来回回答有关警察改革、教育或税收变化的问题。”

contentParagraphs.en: Some in the broader political text messaging industry say generative AI's ability to answer voter's questions and gather data about their concerns is going to be revolutionary for campaigns; others say political text messages are a limited — and annoying — tool and using AI won't improve it. While it's difficult to ascertain how many campaigns are using generative AI to text voters this election cycle, experts say Republicans have been adapting to AI faster than Democrats.
contentParagraphs.zh: 更广泛的政治短信行业的一些人表示，生成式人工智能回答选民问题并收集有关他们担忧的数据的能力将对竞选活动产生革命性影响；其他人则表示，政治短信是一种有限且令人讨厌的工具，使用人工智能并不会改善它。虽然很难确定这个选举周期有多少竞选活动使用生成式人工智能给选民发短信，但专家表示，共和党人比民主党人更快地适应人工智能。

contentParagraphs.en: "My belief is that this is going to make campaigns more interactive, more responsive and more personalized," said Eric Wilson, a Republican strategist and the director of the Center for Campaign Innovation, a nonprofit that encourages conservative campaigns to adopt new technology. He said that generative AI "helps campaigns do more with less."
contentParagraphs.zh: “我的信念是，这将使竞选活动更具互动性，更具响应性和个性化，”共和党战略家兼竞选创新中心主任埃里克·威尔逊（ Eric Wilson ）说。该中心是一家鼓励保守派竞选活动采用新技术的非营利组织。他说，生成式人工智能“帮助广告系列以更少的资源做更多的事情”。

contentParagraphs.en: In almost all cases the first text message sent to voters is written and sent by a human, Wilson said. The AI steps in when the recipient engages.
contentParagraphs.zh: 威尔逊说，在几乎所有情况下，发送给选民的第一条短信都是由人类撰写和发送的。当收件人参与时， AI会介入。

contentParagraphs.en: The era of the long political text message is over, said Tom Carroll, the CEO of Convos, an AI-powered text messaging platform. Convos guides campaigns and their bots to say a sentence, introduce themselves and then ask a question to initiate a conversation.
contentParagraphs.zh: 人工智能短信平台Convos的首席执行官汤姆·卡罗尔（ Tom Carroll ）说，漫长的政治短信时代已经结束。Convos引导营销活动及其机器人说一句话，自我介绍，然后提问以发起对话。

contentParagraphs.en: "What we're offering is the greatest volunteer you've ever had," said Carroll. "They'll respond within 30 seconds, in any language, cutting directly to the question that the person is asking."
contentParagraphs.zh: “我们提供的是你有史以来最伟大的志愿者，”卡罗尔说。“他们将在30秒内以任何语言回复，直接回答对方提出的问题。”

contentParagraphs.en: Convos launched last year, Carroll said, and helped with 10 political campaigns. This year they're aiming to work with over 100 campaigns; so far, they've hit roughly half of that target.
contentParagraphs.zh: 卡罗尔说， Convos去年推出，并帮助开展了10场政治活动。今年，他们的目标是与100多个活动合作；到目前为止，他们已经达到了大约一半的目标。

contentParagraphs.en: Marty Santalucia, a partner at Vector Political, which is focused on generative-AI texting, said bots excel in engaging voters and "in some cases, we have people talk to our agent for hours." About 5-10% of people respond to texts and about 10-20% of those engage in 10 or more texts.
contentParagraphs.zh: 专注于生成式人工智能短信的Vector Political合伙人Marty Santalucia表示，机器人在吸引选民方面表现出色， “在某些情况下，我们让人们与我们的代理商交谈数小时。“大约5-10%的人会回复短信，其中约10-20%的人会回复10条或更多短信。

contentParagraphs.en: "We've sent two and a half million text messages this year and had over 20,000 to 30,000 conversations," said Santalucia. "We're listening at a scale that campaigns have never listened at before."
contentParagraphs.zh: Santalucia说： “我们今年发送了250万条短信，并进行了2万至3万次对话。” “我们正在以竞选活动从未听过的规模进行聆听。”

contentParagraphs.en: The rise of the campaign text message
contentParagraphs.zh: 活动短信的兴起

contentParagraphs.en: The market of political texting greatly expanded in 2020 as candidates were struggling to connect with voters on a grassroots level, said Josh Justice, the CEO of Peerly, a peer-to-peer texting platform. Live phone callers and phone banks diminished as landlines died out. Door-to-door canvassing grew outdated as people grew suspicious of answering the door for strangers. And on social media, the relationship between candidates and voters is owned by the tech platform.
contentParagraphs.zh: Peerly是一个点对点短信平台，其首席执行官乔希·贾斯蒂斯（ Josh Justice ）表示，随着候选人在基层层面努力与选民建立联系，政治短信市场在2020年大幅扩张。随着固定电话的消亡，实时电话呼叫者和电话银行减少了。随着人们越来越怀疑为陌生人开门，挨家挨户的游说变得过时了。在社交媒体上，候选人和选民之间的关系归科技平台所有。

contentParagraphs.en: Justice thinks every single House or Senate campaign this year will send out text messages because it's one of the few ways to reach voters directly on a mass scale. It stays on voters' phones, and it doesn't have to compete with the algorithm for attention.
contentParagraphs.zh: Justice认为，今年的每一次众议院或参议院竞选活动都会发送短信，因为这是直接大规模接触选民的少数几种方式之一。它停留在选民的手机上，不必与算法竞争注意力。

contentParagraphs.en: Justice and others who work for traditional political texting companies said they have ethical concerns with generative AI political text messaging. AI can be used for data analysis or for guiding volunteers, he said, but campaigns should immediately reveal to voters that they are talking to persuasive bots.
contentParagraphs.zh: Justice和其他为传统政治短信公司工作的人表示，他们对生成式人工智能政治短信存在道德问题。他说，人工智能可用于数据分析或指导志愿者，但竞选活动应立即向选民揭示他们正在与有说服力的机器人交谈。

contentParagraphs.en: "I don't think it's ethical to use generative A.I. to communicate with voters," said Justice. "You can put a disclaimer on there, and that's going to make it a lot better. But that defeats the purpose of what everybody started out doing."
contentParagraphs.zh: “我认为使用生成式人工智能与选民沟通是不道德的，” Justice说。“你可以在那里放一个免责声明，这将使它变得更好。但这违背了每个人一开始所做的事情的目的。”

contentParagraphs.en: He is particularly concerned about campaigns using this while laws regulating AI are still being put into place. Campaigns in North Dakota and California have to tell recipients if they are talking to virtual assistants in their first message. Campaigns in New Jersey may soon have to disclose when they use generative AI to provide election-related information to voters.
contentParagraphs.zh: 他特别关注在监管人工智能的法律仍在实施的情况下使用这种方法的运动。北达科他州和加利福尼亚州的活动必须在第一条消息中告知收件人是否正在与虚拟助手交谈。新泽西州的竞选团队在使用生成式人工智能向选民提供与选举相关的信息时，可能很快就必须披露。

contentParagraphs.en: Nathan Rifkin, co-CEO at Scale to Win, a tech company that does grassroots organizing and fundraising for progressives, said the risk of using generative AI outweighs the benefits, including the chatbot giving false information.
contentParagraphs.zh: Scale to Win是一家为基层组织和筹集进步人士资金的科技公司，其联合首席执行官内森·里夫金（ Nathan Rifkin ）表示，使用生成式人工智能的风险超过了收益，包括提供虚假信息的聊天机器人。

contentParagraphs.en: "Or you can lead AI chat bots to say some pretty horrific things," said Rifkin. "If that's in the voice of the candidate, that can lead to some bad ends."
contentParagraphs.zh: “或者你可以让人工智能聊天机器人说一些非常可怕的话，”里夫金说。“如果这是在候选人的声音中，那可能会导致一些不好的结局。”

contentParagraphs.en: Tech companies selling generative AI text messages to political candidates say their clients aren't keen on going public. Some of that is because candidates don't want to share their "secret sauce" said Marty Santalucia from Vector Political. He also admits another element is that it's "very muddy in terms of where public perception is going to fall on this tool."
contentParagraphs.zh: 向政治候选人销售生成式人工智能短信的科技公司表示，他们的客户并不热衷于上市。其中一部分原因是候选人不想分享他们的“秘密酱汁” ， Vector Political的Marty Santalucia说。他还承认，另一个因素是“公众认知将落在这个工具上的地方非常泥泞。”

contentParagraphs.en: A Pew Research Center survey showed Democrats are less confident than Republicans in the government's ability to regulate AI effectively. Democratic campaigns are more hesitant to try out new technology, while Republican campaigns are more energetic about experimentation, Santalucia said.
contentParagraphs.zh: 皮尤研究中心(Pew Research Center)的一项调查显示，民主党人对政府有效监管人工智能的能力的信心低于共和党人。Santalucia说，民主党竞选活动对尝试新技术更加犹豫，而共和党竞选活动对实验更加积极。

contentParagraphs.en: Wilson, who trains Republican campaign strategists and candidate
contentParagraphs.zh: 威尔逊，负责培训共和党竞选战略家和候选人

articles.title: How federal prison guards stop lawsuits before they start
articles.summary: Prisoners are allowed to file complaints about mistreatment — but doing so often comes with retaliation. That's according to an investigation by NPR and The Marshall Project.
articles.titleZh: 联邦监狱看守如何在诉讼开始前阻止诉讼
articles.summaryZh: 囚犯可以对虐待行为提出投诉，但这样做往往会遭到报复。这是根据 NPR 和马歇尔计划的调查得出的。

contentParagraphs.en: Michelle Mildenberg Lara for The Marshall Project
contentParagraphs.zh: 米歇尔·米尔登伯格·劳拉 (Michelle Mildenberg Lara) 为《马歇尔计划》拍摄

contentParagraphs.en: This much is undisputed: On Nov. 2, 2023, a guard and a prisoner at a federal penitentiary in California got into it over a straw sunhat that the officer had confiscated. The man — identified in court records by his initials, J.M. — walked out of the office, as Officer Sandra Munagay followed him. When he stopped and turned around, Munagay "cocked back … and punched me in my face," he said in an interview. That is on camera. Munagay admitted to the assault and pleaded guilty this January to falsifying records about it.
contentParagraphs.zh: 这是无可争议的：2023 年 11 月 2 日，加利福尼亚州一所联邦监狱的一名看守和一名囚犯因警察没收的一顶草帽而进入监狱。法庭记录显示，这名男子的名字缩写是 J.M.，他走出办公室，警官桑德拉·穆纳盖 (Sandra Munagay) 尾随其后。当他停下来转身时，穆纳盖“向后退了一步……并打了我的脸”，他在接受采访时说道。那是在相机上。穆纳盖承认了这起袭击事件，并于今年一月承认伪造相关记录的罪名。

contentParagraphs.en: But the more severe harm came after, J.M. said, in a hallway without security cameras. As Munagay kicked and hit him, she shouted to other officers that J.M. had attacked her. According to a lawsuit, at least three other guards then rushed in, forced him into a blind spot, and pinned him face-first to a wall. With J.M.'s hands cuffed, he says an officer then sexually assaulted him with an unknown object.
contentParagraphs.zh: 但 J.M. 说，更严重的伤害发生在没有安全摄像头的走廊里。当穆纳盖踢打他时，她向其他警官大喊，说 J.M. 袭击了她。根据诉讼，至少另外三名警卫随后冲了进来，将他逼到了盲点，并将他的脸朝下按在墙上。 J.M. 的双手被戴上手铐，一名警官随后用不明物体对他进行了性侵犯。

contentParagraphs.en: That night, J.M. was transferred to another prison, where a nurse noted bleeding and tenderness in his rectum, medical records show. That gave J.M. more proof than most people behind bars in his situation.
contentParagraphs.zh: 医疗记录显示，当晚，J.M. 被转移到另一所监狱，一名护士注意到他的直肠有出血和压痛。这给了 J.M. 比大多数监狱里的人更多的证据。

contentParagraphs.en: But guards still had near-total control over whether he could file a complaint, or someday sue over what happened to him. J.M. knew they could destroy his paperwork, claim it got lost, or simply deny him the forms he needed. And like he had experienced in other federal prisons, he says, they might punish him for even trying to speak out.
contentParagraphs.zh: 但警卫仍然几乎完全控制着他是否可以提出投诉，或者有一天对他所发生的事情提起诉讼。 J.M. 知道他们可能会销毁他的文件，声称文件丢失，或者干脆拒绝提供他所需的表格。他说，就像他在其他联邦监狱所经历的那样，他们可能会因为他试图发声而惩罚他。

contentParagraphs.en: It's the same dilemma presented to anyone who faces violence in federal prison: Try to file an administrative grievance and risk opening yourself up to retaliation — or stay quiet, endure the abuse, and forgo your chance to someday bring your case to court.
contentParagraphs.zh: 对于任何在联邦监狱中面临暴力的人来说，这都是同样的困境：尝试提出行政申诉，并冒着遭受报复的风险——或者保持沉默，忍受虐待，并放弃有一天将案件告上法庭的机会。

contentParagraphs.en: Under federal law, people in prison must go through the facility's own grievance process before they can attempt to sue. That gives prison staff a "chokehold over access to the courts," said Colin Prince, a civil rights attorney and former federal defender who is representing J.M. in his lawsuit.
contentParagraphs.zh: 根据联邦法律，监狱里的人必须先经过监狱自己的申诉程序，然后才能尝试起诉。这让监狱工作人员“无法诉诸法庭”，在诉讼中代表 J.M. 的民权律师、前联邦辩护人科林·普林斯 (Colin Prince) 说。

contentParagraphs.en: "The guards functionally have power over whether a prisoner can sue them for their own misconduct," he said. "The entire system is layer upon layer of bureaucratic insulation against accountability. It simply prevents prisoners from getting access to the courts."
contentParagraphs.zh: “狱警实际上有权决定囚犯是否可以因自己的不当行为而起诉他们，”他说。 “整个系统是层层官僚隔离，不利于问责。它只是阻止囚犯诉诸法庭。”

contentParagraphs.en: An attorney for Munagay said he and his client declined to comment. A spokesperson for the Bureau of Prisons, Randilee Giamusso, said she could not discuss individual cases or ongoing litigation.
contentParagraphs.zh: 穆纳盖的一名律师表示，他和他的当事人拒绝发表评论。监狱管理局发言人兰迪莉·吉亚穆索 (Randilee Giamusso) 表示，她无法讨论个别案件或正在进行的诉讼。

contentParagraphs.en: An investigation by The Marshall Project and NPR found that less than 2% of grievances filed in federal prison in 2023 were granted. A majority were rejected for procedural errors or "administratively closed" for other reasons. The findings were based on a federal database, published by the Data Liberation Project, containing nearly 1 million federal prison grievance cases dating back to 2000.
contentParagraphs.zh: 马歇尔计划和 NPR 的一项调查发现，2023 年向联邦监狱提出的申诉中只有不到 2% 得到批准。大多数因程序错误而被拒绝，或因其他原因“行政关闭”。调查结果基于数据解放项目发布的联邦数据库，其中包含自 2000 年以来近 100 万个联邦监狱申诉案件。

contentParagraphs.en: But that data only includes instances where incarcerated people were able to file a complaint at all. An unknown number of cases, especially those involving physical and sexual violence, go unreported, as the same officers accused of abuse can silence those trying to seek help, according to court records, lawsuits, and interviews with attorneys, incarcerated people, advocates and former bureau officials. A recent report by the Government Accountability Office found that fear of retaliation was a major impediment to reducing and reporting sexual abuse in federal prisons.
contentParagraphs.zh: 但这些数据仅包括被监禁者能够提出投诉的情况。根据法庭记录、诉讼以及对律师、被监禁者、倡导者和前局官员的采访，数量不详的案件，特别是涉及身体暴力和性暴力的案件没有得到报道，因为被指控虐待的警察可以让那些试图寻求帮助的人保持沉默。政府问责办公室最近的一份报告发现，对报复的恐惧是减少和报告联邦监狱性虐待行为的主要障碍。

contentParagraphs.en: Prison officials said bureau policy prohibits retaliation of any kind, and that they review and investigate allegations of abuse. In an email, Giamusso wrote that the remedy system is "a safeguard intended to foster resolution within the system, not a barrier to court access." She noted that remedies related to sexual abuse can be submitted in other ways, such as "third-party reporting and [Prison Rape Elimination Act]-specific channels."
contentParagraphs.zh: 监狱官员表示，该局的政策禁止任何形式的报复，他们会审查和调查虐待指控。贾穆索在一封电子邮件中写道，补救制度是“旨在促进系统内解决问题的保障措施，而不是进入法庭的障碍。”她指出，与性虐待有关的补救措施可以通过其他方式提交，例如“第三方报告和[监狱强奸消除法]特定渠道”。

contentParagraphs.en: But many prisoners disagree. "The grievance system is a joke," said Jimmy Hodge, who was released from federal prison in early 2025. Hodge says he was abused in multiple federal penitentiaries, but was frequently blocked from filing complaints about it. "If you're grieving over abuse, they're going to harass you, they're going to assault you, but you're never going to get relief."
contentParagraphs.zh: 但许多囚犯不同意。 2025 年初从联邦监狱获释的吉米·霍奇 (Jimmy Hodge) 说：“申诉制度就是个笑话。”霍奇说，他在多个联邦监狱中受到虐待，但经常被禁止提出投诉。 “如果你对虐待感到悲伤，他们就会骚扰你，他们会攻击你，但你永远不会得到缓解。”

contentParagraphs.en: Since the passage of the Prison Litigation Reform Act 30 years ago, which required incarcerated people to file grievances before attempting to sue, the rate of civil rights cases filed from prison has dropped significantly.
contentParagraphs.zh: 30年前《监狱诉讼改革法》通过，要求被监禁者在试图起诉前提出申诉，自此以来，从监狱提起的民权案件的比例大幅下降。

contentParagraphs.en: Lawmakers at the time were concerned about "frivolous" lawsuits from prisons overwhelming federal courts. Politicians pointed to one case where a person had allegedly sued over whether he received chunky or creamy peanut butter. (The case was actually about not getting a $2.50 refund for peanut butter returned to the commissary, which is the equivalent of hours of prison labor.)
contentParagraphs.zh: 当时的立法者担心监狱的“无聊”诉讼压倒了联邦法院。政客们提到了一起案件，据称有人因收到的是厚实的还是奶油状的花生酱而提起诉讼。 （此案实际上是关于退回小卖部的花生酱未获得 2.5 美元退款，这相当于监狱劳动时间。）

contentParagraphs.en: "People talk a lot about prisoners filing frivolous lawsuits," said professor Margo Schlanger of the University of Michigan Law School, who has studied prison litigation across the U.S. "But a huge number of prisoner cases are about really, really serious matters. They're about abysmal medical care and awful conditions and failures to protect them from harm by staff or by other prisoners. They're about sexual violations."
contentParagraphs.zh: 研究美国各地监狱诉讼的密歇根大学法学院教授玛戈·施兰格表示：“人们经常谈论囚犯提起无意义的诉讼。但大量囚犯案件涉及非常非常严重的问题。它们涉及糟糕的医疗保健和恶劣的条件，以及未能保护他们免受工作人员或其他囚犯伤害的问题。它们涉及性侵犯。”

contentParagraphs.en: Attempts to significantly reform the law have gone nowhere, Schlanger said. "Having a system that stands in the way and says, 'You know what, because you filed that grievance after three days instead of after two, you are out of luck and out of court' — that is a shocking betrayal of justice."
contentParagraphs.zh: 施兰格表示，对法律进行重大改革的尝试毫无进展。 “有一个系统阻碍并说，‘你知道吗，因为你是在三天而不是两天后提出申诉，所以你运气不好，被法庭起诉’——这是对正义的令人震惊的背叛。”

contentParagraphs.en: People who are blocked from filing grievances can sometimes convince a court that the remedy system was unavailable and their lawsuit should proceed. But that is a high bar that may require documentation and the help of an attorney, which many people filing from prison don't have.
contentParagraphs.zh: 被阻止提出申诉的人有时可以说服法院，补救制度不可用，他们的诉讼应该继续进行。但这是一个很高的门槛，可能需要文件和律师的帮助，而许多从监狱提出申请的人都没有这些。

contentParagraphs.en: As is, the law fails to acc
contentParagraphs.zh: 事实上，法律未能遵循
